title: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension
oneLiner: IIH is raised ICP without mass/ infection, classically in obese women of childbearing age; vision loss is the main threat.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis (CVST)** – mimics every feature. **Key finding:** CT/MR venogram shows filling defect; absence of papilloedema doesn't rule it out. **Action:** NEVER do LP before excluding CVST.
- **Malignant Hypertension** – can cause papilloedema & headache. **Key finding:** BP >180/120 + proteinuria or end-organ damage. **Action:** Check BP immediately; treat hypertensive emergency before any LP.
- **Space‑occupying lesion / hydrocephalus** – both cause raised ICP. **Key finding:** CT head shows mass, midline shift, or ventricular enlargement. **Action:** Urgent neuroimaging before LP.
sections.Presentation:
- **Headache:** daily, non‑pulsatile, worse lying down or on coughing; may wake patient
- **Transient visual obscurations:** brief (<30 sec) blackouts — often postural
- **Pulsatile tinnitus:** unilateral or bilateral, synchronous with pulse
- **Diplopia:** usually horizontal, due to sixth nerve palsy (false localising sign)
- **Blurred vision / visual field loss:** peripheral field defects typical
- **Exam:** papilloedema on fundoscopy (bilateral in >90%); ± sixth nerve palsy; visual acuity often normal early
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **CVST** – venogram required; suspect if focal neurology, seizure, or hypercoagulable state
- **Migraine / chronic daily headache** – no papilloedema; normal imaging & opening pressure
- **Space‑occupying lesion** – focal signs, structural lesion on CT/MRI
- **Meningitis** – fever, neck stiffness, CSF pleocytosis
- **CSF leak / low pressure headache** – orthostatic headache (worse upright) vs IIH (worse lying)
- **Splitter:** **Papilloedema + normal MRI brain + open venous sinuses → IIH likely**; if venogram abnormal → CVST
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** fundoscopy, visual acuity (Snellen chart), confrontation visual fields; check BP; weight + BMI
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation screen, ESR/CRP, pregnancy test (β‑hCG) — **all IIH patients of childbearing age MUST have pregnancy test**
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First‑line:** CT head + CT venogram (or MRI brain + MR venogram) – to exclude mass, hydrocephalus, CVST  
  - **Second‑line:** MRI brain with contrast (if CT equivocal)
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Lumbar puncture:** **must** have normal imaging first. Measure opening pressure (normal <25 cm H₂O; IIH >25, often >35). Send CSF: cell count, protein, glucose, culture, cytology – to rule out infection / malignancy  
  - **Formal visual field testing (Humphrey / Goldmann):** baseline and for monitoring  
  - **Ophthalmology review:** for papilloedema grading, optic disc photos
- **Diagnostic criteria (modified Dandy criteria):**  
  - Symptoms of raised ICP (headache, visual obscurations, tinnitus)  
  - Signs: papilloedema, ± sixth nerve palsy  
  - Normal neuroimaging (no mass, no hydrocephalus, no CVST)  
  - LP opening pressure >25 cm H₂O with normal CSF composition
- **Never‑miss test:** **CT/MR venogram** – missing CVST → delayed anticoagulation → catastrophic stroke
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If visual acuity suddenly drops → **call ophthalmology & neurology immediately**; consider high‑dose acetazolamide or urgent CSF drainage  
  - If BP >180/120 → treat hypertensive emergency (e.g. labetalol IV) before LP  
  - Once imaging negative, **perform diagnostic LP** (therapeutic drainage of 20–30 mL CSF can relieve headache)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Start **acetazolamide** (see Drugs & Doses)  
  - Formal visual fields + ophthalmology review daily if visual concerns  
  - **Weight management referral** (dietitian, bariatric MDT) – only proven disease‑modifying therapy  
  - Pain control for headache (paracetamol, avoid opioids that cause constipation / drowsiness)
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**  
  - Acetazolamide wean after >6 months if symptoms + papilloedema resolved  
  - **Target weight loss:** 5–10% body weight significantly reduces ICP  
  - Consider **topiramate** (second‑line, also aids weight loss)  
  - If refractory vision loss → CSF shunting (LP shunt, ventriculoperitoneal shunt) or optic nerve sheath fenestration
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: no restriction unless dehydrated – aim for euvolaemia  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (obesity increases risk)  
  - Diet: as tolerated; encourage low‑calorie if able  
  - Observations: routine NEWS2, daily weight, daily visual acuity + fields
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line:** **Acetazolamide**  
  - Starting dose: 250 mg PO twice daily; increase to 500 mg twice daily as tolerated (max 1 g daily) – **[check local policy]**
  - Duration: usually 6–12 months; taper slowly
  - Contraindications: sulfonamide allergy, severe renal impairment, pregnancy (risk of metabolic acidosis)
  - Interactions: potentiates diuretics, lithium, hypoglycaemics
  - **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** avoid if CrCl <30 mL/min; no hepatic dose adjustment needed
- **Common alternative:** **Topiramate**  
  - Starting dose: 25 mg PO once daily, titrate weekly to 100–200 mg daily in two divided doses – **[check local policy]**
  - Contraindications: pregnancy (teratogenic), history of nephrolithiasis
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Acetazolamide – causing severe metabolic acidosis** (monitor bicarbonate; reduce dose if bicarbonate <18 mmol/L). Also: **pregnancy test before starting** – never give in pregnancy without specialist advice.
- **Other:** **Furosemide** (rarely used as second‑line) – avoid if dehydrated.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Rapid visual loss (central retinal vein occlusion, optic atrophy) – permanent if not treated within hours  
  - Sixth nerve palsy (transient, usually resolves)  
  - LP complications: post‑dural puncture headache, spinal haematoma
- **Chronic:**  
  - Irreversible visual field loss (up to 10% of patients)  
  - Chronic headache (can persist despite normal ICP)  
  - Optic atrophy  
  - Weight gain / metabolic effects of acetazolamide
sections.Prognosis: - Vision improves in 90% if treated early; 5–10% have permanent field defects. Relapse common if weight not lost. Mortality low (mainly from untreated CVST or shunt infection).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do formal visual fields at baseline** – acuity can be normal despite severe field loss.  
- **Encourage weight loss aggressively** – it’s the only disease‑modifying therapy; consider bariatric referral early.  
- **Use therapeutic LP as treatment** – draining 20–30 mL CSF provides immediate headache relief and confirms diagnosis.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT perform LP before imaging** – risk of brain herniation if mass / hydrocephalus.  
- **Do NOT forget to exclude CVST** – 10% of suspected IIH actually have CVST; missing it leads to stroke.  
- **Do NOT give corticosteroids** – they worsen obesity, do not reduce ICP in genuine IIH, and may mask underlying infection.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Sudden vision loss / constant blurring  
  - Worsening headache not relieved by simple analgesia  
  - New neurological symptoms (weakness, seizure, confusion)  
- **Follow‑up:**  
  - Neurology or ophthalmology clinic in 2–4 weeks  
  - Repeat visual fields and fundoscopy at every visit  
  - Weight management clinic within 3 months
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / neurology SpR immediately if:**  
  - Visual acuity drops rapidly (<6/60) or new visual field defect  
  - GCS <15 or focal neurology (suggests alternative diagnosis)  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 (suspect sepsis / CVST)  
  - Severe headache unresponsive to LP  
- **Call crash team / ITU if:**  
  - GCS ≤8 or seizures  
  - Respiratory depression from raised ICP (rare in IIH)  
  - Post‑LP coning (if LP done incorrectly)

rawMarkdown:
# Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH)

**One-liner:** IIH is raised ICP without mass/ infection, classically in obese women of childbearing age; vision loss is the main threat.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis (CVST)** – mimics every feature. **Key finding:** CT/MR venogram shows filling defect; absence of papilloedema doesn't rule it out. **Action:** NEVER do LP before excluding CVST.
- **Malignant Hypertension** – can cause papilloedema & headache. **Key finding:** BP >180/120 + proteinuria or end-organ damage. **Action:** Check BP immediately; treat hypertensive emergency before any LP.
- **Space‑occupying lesion / hydrocephalus** – both cause raised ICP. **Key finding:** CT head shows mass, midline shift, or ventricular enlargement. **Action:** Urgent neuroimaging before LP.

### Presentation
- **Headache:** daily, non‑pulsatile, worse lying down or on coughing; may wake patient
- **Transient visual obscurations:** brief (<30 sec) blackouts — often postural
- **Pulsatile tinnitus:** unilateral or bilateral, synchronous with pulse
- **Diplopia:** usually horizontal, due to sixth nerve palsy (false localising sign)
- **Blurred vision / visual field loss:** peripheral field defects typical
- **Exam:** papilloedema on fundoscopy (bilateral in >90%); ± sixth nerve palsy; visual acuity often normal early

### Differential Diagnosis
- **CVST** – venogram required; suspect if focal neurology, seizure, or hypercoagulable state
- **Migraine / chronic daily headache** – no papilloedema; normal imaging & opening pressure
- **Space‑occupying lesion** – focal signs, structural lesion on CT/MRI
- **Meningitis** – fever, neck stiffness, CSF pleocytosis
- **CSF leak / low pressure headache** – orthostatic headache (worse upright) vs IIH (worse lying)
- **Splitter:** **Papilloedema + normal MRI brain + open venous sinuses → IIH likely**; if venogram abnormal → CVST

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** fundoscopy, visual acuity (Snellen chart), confrontation visual fields; check BP; weight + BMI
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation screen, ESR/CRP, pregnancy test (β‑hCG) — **all IIH patients of childbearing age MUST have pregnancy test**
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First‑line:** CT head + CT venogram (or MRI brain + MR venogram) – to exclude mass, hydrocephalus, CVST  
  - **Second‑line:** MRI brain with contrast (if CT equivocal)
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Lumbar puncture:** **must** have normal imaging first. Measure opening pressure (normal <25 cm H₂O; IIH >25, often >35). Send CSF: cell count, protein, glucose, culture, cytology – to rule out infection / malignancy  
  - **Formal visual field testing (Humphrey / Goldmann):** baseline and for monitoring  
  - **Ophthalmology review:** for papilloedema grading, optic disc photos
- **Diagnostic criteria (modified Dandy criteria):**  
  - Symptoms of raised ICP (headache, visual obscurations, tinnitus)  
  - Signs: papilloedema, ± sixth nerve palsy  
  - Normal neuroimaging (no mass, no hydrocephalus, no CVST)  
  - LP opening pressure >25 cm H₂O with normal CSF composition
- **Never‑miss test:** **CT/MR venogram** – missing CVST → delayed anticoagulation → catastrophic stroke

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If visual acuity suddenly drops → **call ophthalmology & neurology immediately**; consider high‑dose acetazolamide or urgent CSF drainage  
  - If BP >180/120 → treat hypertensive emergency (e.g. labetalol IV) before LP  
  - Once imaging negative, **perform diagnostic LP** (therapeutic drainage of 20–30 mL CSF can relieve headache)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Start **acetazolamide** (see Drugs & Doses)  
  - Formal visual fields + ophthalmology review daily if visual concerns  
  - **Weight management referral** (dietitian, bariatric MDT) – only proven disease‑modifying therapy  
  - Pain control for headache (paracetamol, avoid opioids that cause constipation / drowsiness)
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**  
  - Acetazolamide wean after >6 months if symptoms + papilloedema resolved  
  - **Target weight loss:** 5–10% body weight significantly reduces ICP  
  - Consider **topiramate** (second‑line, also aids weight loss)  
  - If refractory vision loss → CSF shunting (LP shunt, ventriculoperitoneal shunt) or optic nerve sheath fenestration
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: no restriction unless dehydrated – aim for euvolaemia  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (obesity increases risk)  
  - Diet: as tolerated; encourage low‑calorie if able  
  - Observations: routine NEWS2, daily weight, daily visual acuity + fields

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line:** **Acetazolamide**  
  - Starting dose: 250 mg PO twice daily; increase to 500 mg twice daily as tolerated (max 1 g daily) – **[check local policy]**
  - Duration: usually 6–12 months; taper slowly
  - Contraindications: sulfonamide allergy, severe renal impairment, pregnancy (risk of metabolic acidosis)
  - Interactions: potentiates diuretics, lithium, hypoglycaemics
  - **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** avoid if CrCl <30 mL/min; no hepatic dose adjustment needed
- **Common alternative:** **Topiramate**  
  - Starting dose: 25 mg PO once daily, titrate weekly to 100–200 mg daily in two divided doses – **[check local policy]**
  - Contraindications: pregnancy (teratogenic), history of nephrolithiasis
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Acetazolamide – causing severe metabolic acidosis** (monitor bicarbonate; reduce dose if bicarbonate <18 mmol/L). Also: **pregnancy test before starting** – never give in pregnancy without specialist advice.
- **Other:** **Furosemide** (rarely used as second‑line) – avoid if dehydrated.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Rapid visual loss (central retinal vein occlusion, optic atrophy) – permanent if not treated within hours  
  - Sixth nerve palsy (transient, usually resolves)  
  - LP complications: post‑dural puncture headache, spinal haematoma
- **Chronic:**  
  - Irreversible visual field loss (up to 10% of patients)  
  - Chronic headache (can persist despite normal ICP)  
  - Optic atrophy  
  - Weight gain / metabolic effects of acetazolamide

### Prognosis
- Vision improves in 90% if treated early; 5–10% have permanent field defects. Relapse common if weight not lost. Mortality low (mainly from untreated CVST or shunt infection).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do formal visual fields at baseline** – acuity can be normal despite severe field loss.  
- **Encourage weight loss aggressively** – it’s the only disease‑modifying therapy; consider bariatric referral early.  
- **Use therapeutic LP as treatment** – draining 20–30 mL CSF provides immediate headache relief and confirms diagnosis.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT perform LP before imaging** – risk of brain herniation if mass / hydrocephalus.  
- **Do NOT forget to exclude CVST** – 10% of suspected IIH actually have CVST; missing it leads to stroke.  
- **Do NOT give corticosteroids** – they worsen obesity, do not reduce ICP in genuine IIH, and may mask underlying infection.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Sudden vision loss / constant blurring  
  - Worsening headache not relieved by simple analgesia  
  - New neurological symptoms (weakness, seizure, confusion)  
- **Follow‑up:**  
  - Neurology or ophthalmology clinic in 2–4 weeks  
  - Repeat visual fields and fundoscopy at every visit  
  - Weight management clinic within 3 months

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / neurology SpR immediately if:**  
  - Visual acuity drops rapidly (<6/60) or new visual field defect  
  - GCS <15 or focal neurology (suggests alternative diagnosis)  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 (suspect sepsis / CVST)  
  - Severe headache unresponsive to LP  
- **Call crash team / ITU if:**  
  - GCS ≤8 or seizures  
  - Respiratory depression from raised ICP (rare in IIH)  
  - Post‑LP coning (if LP done incorrectly)
